desc: A gap between what's expected and what's real. The engine underneath most other comedy.

subs.def: Reality turns out the opposite of what's expected.
subs.ex: A fire station burns down.

subs.def: You say the opposite of what you mean.
subs.ex: You spill coffee on yourself: “Great, just what I needed.”

subs.def: The audience knows something the character doesn't.
subs.ex: In Friends, we know Monica & Chandler are together; everyone's obliviousness IS the joke.

subs.def: Verbal irony with a sharp, mocking edge — the contempt is the point.
subs.ex: “Oh wow, another Monday. My absolute favourite.”

desc: Archetypes — 2D personalities with 1–3 fixed traits. The joke fires when they act exactly as their archetype demands.

subs.def: Confidently wrong about everything.
subs.ex: Homer Simpson, Kevin from The Office.

subs.def: Looks down on everyone, constantly.
subs.ex: Frasier Crane.

subs.def: A completely normal person surrounded by chaos — the relatable straight-man.
subs.ex: Jim Halpert.

subs.def: Always correcting everyone, usually right, always annoying.
subs.ex: Sheldon Cooper.

subs.def: Defined entirely by romantic pursuit.
subs.ex: Barney Stinson.

subs.def: Social awkwardness as personality.
subs.ex: Leonard Hofstadter.

subs.def: No emotional understanding, hyper-logical.
subs.ex: Spock, Sheldon.

subs.def: Innocent, childlike, no idea how the world works.
subs.ex: Buddy the Elf, Kimmy Schmidt.

subs.def: Well-meaning but destroys everything they touch.
subs.ex: Michael Scott.

desc: Humor from crossing a line — content that appears where you don't expect it.

subs.def: Explicit or suggestive content where you don't expect it.
subs.ex: Most of Superbad.

subs.def: Casual mention of harm or death for comic effect.
subs.ex: “I hit a kid with my car. Thankfully no one saw it.” — Jeselnik

subs.def: Unexpected swearing in a formal or serious context amplifies impact.
subs.ex: A professor calmly saying “this formula is absolute bullshit” mid-lecture.

subs.def: Bodily functions, disgusting imagery, physical repulsion.
subs.ex: Most of Family Guy.

subs.def: Death, racism, religion, disability — treated lightly when convention says you shouldn't.
subs.ex: Most of Curb Your Enthusiasm's premises.

desc: Comic exaggeration — push the scale until the size itself is the joke.

subs.def: Make something comically enormous or tiny.
subs.ex: “I've told you a thousand million times.”

subs.def: Defies physics — the impossibility is the whole joke.
subs.ex: “My doctor tested my ear and it had an olive in it.” — Rodney Dangerfield

subs.def: Inflated, royal-sounding language for mundane things.
subs.ex: “Magnanimous Boss, King of Kings, Allows Employees to Watch World Cup.”

subs.def: Made-up or wildly inflated numbers stated with total confidence.
subs.ex: “Miracle of Birth Occurs for 83 Billionth Time.”

desc: Humor from language itself — sound, structure, and double meanings, not association.

subs.def: Same sound, two meanings.
subs.ex: “I used to be a banker but I lost interest.”

subs.def: A phrase with one innocent meaning and one… not innocent. Intentionally ambiguous.
subs.ex: The engine of countless sitcom one-liners.

subs.def: Two contradictory words together, played for absurdity.
subs.ex: “Deafening silence.” “Organised chaos.”

subs.def: Using the wrong word that sounds similar — character sounds like an idiot.
subs.ex: “For all intensive purposes.” (Michael Scott does this constantly.)

subs.def: Smashing two words into one new word.
subs.ex: “Fritolaysia” (Frito-Lay + Malaysia). Brunch. Snark.

subs.def: Swapping the first sounds of two words accidentally.
subs.ex: “You have hissed all my mystery lectures.”

subs.def: Humor from the sound itself, not just meaning.
subs.ex: “Boaty McBoatface.”

subs.def: Taking a figurative expression completely literally.
subs.ex: Someone says “break a leg” and the character genuinely tries to.

subs.def: Dropping a wildly out-of-place word into an otherwise normal sentence.
subs.ex: “The senator, resplendent in his khakis, committed several war crimes.”

subs.def: Making up completely fictional words with total confidence.
subs.ex: Monty Python does this constantly. So does 30 Rock.

desc: Humor that borrows recognition — pointing at something the audience already knows.

subs.def: Referencing a known film, song, meme, or moment.
subs.ex: HIMYM's entire structure is one long pop culture callback engine.

subs.def: Using a known historical event in an unexpected context.
subs.ex: A grand historical framing applied to something trivial.

subs.def: Referencing the rules of a genre so the audience feels in on it.
subs.ex: A horror character saying “I'll be right back” — everyone knows what that means.

subs.def: Only a specific group gets it. Makes that group feel special.
subs.ex: Big Bang Theory's actual physics jokes — funnier because you're not supposed to get them.

subs.def: Things everyone has lived through. Instant recognition = instant connection.
subs.ex: Alarm goes off. You hit snooze 9 times. You're now late. Everyone nods.

desc: Absurdist logic — reality is bent and nobody in the scene seems to notice.

subs.def: A response or statement with zero logical connection to what came before.
subs.ex: Someone asks “What time is it?” and the answer is “Purple.”

subs.def: Physical comedy where cause-and-effect is exaggerated beyond reality.
subs.ex: Tom and Jerry. Wile E. Coyote. The cartoon physics ARE the joke.

subs.def: The entire premise defies reality and no one acknowledges it.
subs.ex: The Mighty Boosh — a man turns into a living chair. Nobody mentions it.

subs.def: Two wildly unrelated things placed side by side.
subs.ex: “I enjoy long walks on the beach and spreadsheet auditing.”

desc: Mimicking a form, then exaggerating or collapsing it.

subs.def: Mimicking an entire genre's conventions and exaggerating them.
subs.ex: Scary Movie (horror). Airplane! (disaster films).

subs.def: Copying the tone and aesthetic of a specific creator or format.
subs.ex: A nature documentary voice-over narrating someone making a sandwich.

subs.def: Setting up a familiar trope and then collapsing it.
subs.ex: The hero gives an inspiring speech. It changes nothing. Everyone goes home.

subs.def: Using the visual or structural format of something serious for something absurd.
subs.ex: The Onion's entire existence — newspaper format, utterly unhinged content.

subs.def: Targeting one specific film, show, or person.
subs.ex: “Al Gore Places Infant Son in Rocket” directly spoofs Superman's origin story.

desc: Comedy from comparison — yoking two things whose similarity you didn't see coming.

subs.def: “X is like Y” where Y is totally unexpected.
subs.ex: “Marriage is like a software update — you don't want it, but eventually you have no choice.”

subs.def: Stretching a comparison across an entire bit.
subs.ex: Treating a corporate office as a medieval kingdom — boss is the king, HR is the church, the printer is the village idiot — and never breaking it.

subs.def: Finding a genuine similarity between two things with no business being similar.
subs.ex: “Raising a toddler is exactly like managing a drunk, hostile CEO.”

subs.def: Treating two wildly unequal things as identical (played for laughs).
subs.ex: “Losing your charger and losing a family member — both equally devastating.”

desc: The comedy of attention pointed at exactly the wrong thing.

subs.def: The elephant in the room is enormous. The character notices the carpet instead.
subs.ex: A building is on fire. A character is annoyed the fire alarm interrupted their podcast.

subs.def: A genuinely terrible situation is treated as a minor inconvenience.
subs.ex: “Yes the Titanic is sinking, but did anyone else notice the soup was cold at dinner?”

subs.def: In a crisis, the character focuses on something completely beside the point.
subs.ex: Jeselnik's car/kid joke — the tragedy is real; his concern is: witnesses.

subs.def: Appearing to care about something, but the thing you care about reveals you're actually a monster.
subs.ex: “I'm not a bad person — I cried at the end of that commercial.” (The commercial was for car insurance.)

desc: Comedy that is aware of itself — the joke points back at the joke.

subs.def: Character acknowledges they're in a story, show, or joke.
subs.ex: Ferris Bueller talking to camera. Deadpool referencing his own franchise.

subs.def: The joke is about itself or its own structure.
subs.ex: “That's what she said” — Michael Scott making a joke about making a joke.

subs.def: Making the mechanics of the joke itself visible.
subs.ex: A comedian pausing to explain why a joke works, mid-joke, while still making it funny.

subs.def: Comedy that targets the idea of comedy.
subs.ex: “That joke is so old it voted against women's suffrage.”
